enrichment.short_description: Messaging-first CRM (formerly amoCRM) that unifies WhatsApp, Instagram and email into one sales pipeline with no-code bots and AI agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Kommo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kommo (formerly amoCRM) is a messaging-first CRM with unified inbox, Salesbot automation and AI agents. Compare its pricing, features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kommo is a messaging-centric customer relationship management platform originally launched in 2009 as amoCRM and officially rebranded to Kommo in October 2022, with the underlying product, engineering team and pricing structure carried over unchanged through the rename. The company presents its headquarters as San Francisco, California, and positions itself globally, serving small and mid-sized sales teams across more than 100 countries with localized versions in languages including Spanish, Portuguese, Russian, German, French and Arabic. Architecturally, Kommo is built around a unified multichannel inbox that funnels conversations from WhatsApp, Instagram Direct, Facebook Messenger, Telegram, email and web chat widgets into a single threaded view tied to a contact or deal record, so sales reps never have to switch between separate messaging apps to manage a lead. On top of that inbox sits a classic visual sales pipeline with drag-and-drop kanban-style deal cards, customizable pipeline stages, task reminders and lead scoring, plus a no-code automation builder called Salesbot that lets non-developers construct branching conversational flows for lead qualification, follow-up sequences and appointment reminders without writing code. In 2024 and 2025 Kommo layered generative AI on top of this messaging core with an AI Agent feature: configurable AI-driven assistants that can answer customer questions, summarize conversations, suggest replies and tasks to reps, and in higher tiers operate semi-autonomously across up to 50 concurrent agents per account. Kommo's pricing is structured as four tiers billed per user with a minimum six-month commitment: Base at $15 per user per month includes the core workspace, sales pipelines, unified inbox, 2,500 active leads per user, one WhatsApp number and Instagram account, and up to three AI agents with basic reply/task suggestions; Advanced at $25 per user per month adds Salesbot automation, broadcast messaging, 5,000 active leads per user and three WhatsApp/Instagram accounts; Pro at $45 per user per month (marketed as the most popular tier) unlocks booking and scheduling tools, unlimited channels and WhatsApp numbers, 10,000 active leads per user, a fuller AI kit with up to 50 AI agents, and sales and marketing analytics; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier adds enterprise security, single sign-on, a dedicated account manager and priority support. Every plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required and full feature access, capped only by 10GB of media storage during the trial period. Kommo also ships native mobile apps for iOS and Android that include a business-card scanner for capturing leads at events, along with a broader ecosystem of integrations covering payment gateways, e-commerce platforms, Google Workspace, and hundreds of third-party apps via its marketplace and API. The platform targets small and mid-market sales, marketing and support teams — particularly those doing high volumes of business over messaging apps such as WhatsApp and Instagram in markets like Latin America, the Middle East and Southeast Asia where chat-based commerce is dominant, as well as agencies, real estate brokerages, e-commerce sellers, and B2B sales teams that want pipeline management fused directly with conversational channels rather than bolted on as an afterthought. Kommo's most direct competitors are other CRM platforms with strong messaging or automation angles: HubSpot CRM and Pipedrive compete on broader marketing and sales-suite functionality with steeper enterprise pricing; Zoho CRM and Bitrix24 compete on breadth of bundled business tools at a lower price point; and messaging-specific competitors like Freshsales and Close focus on similar chat-driven pipelines. Kommo differentiates itself primarily through the depth of its native WhatsApp and Instagram integration (rather than requiring third-party connectors), its no-code Salesbot builder aimed at non-technical sales managers, and its early, tightly integrated push into AI agents built specifically for messenger-based selling rather than generic AI add-ons retrofitted onto a traditional form-and-field CRM.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kommo, known for over a decade as amoCRM before its 2022 rebrand, is a customer relationship management platform built around the idea that most sales conversations now happen inside messaging apps rather than email. Instead of treating WhatsApp, Instagram and web chat as bolted-on channels, Kommo routes every conversation into a single unified inbox tied to the underlying deal and contact record, so a sales rep sees the full conversation history alongside pipeline status, tasks and notes in one place.

The product is organized around a visual, kanban-style sales pipeline that reps can customize to match their own sales process, combined with a no-code automation builder (Salesbot) for constructing chatbot-driven qualification and follow-up flows, and a newer AI Agent layer that can draft replies, summarize threads and handle parts of a conversation autonomously. This combination makes Kommo popular with teams that do a large share of their selling over chat apps rather than phone or email.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kommo's core feature set centers on its unified inbox, which merges WhatsApp, Instagram Direct, Facebook Messenger, Telegram, email and website chat into one thread per contact, eliminating the need to check multiple apps to manage a single lead. Around that inbox sits a drag-and-drop sales pipeline with customizable stages, deal cards, task automation and lead-scoring rules that let managers see exactly where every deal stands.

Salesbot, Kommo's no-code automation builder, lets non-technical users design branching chatbot flows for lead qualification, appointment booking and re-engagement campaigns without writing code. The newer AI Agent capability builds on top of this by generating reply suggestions, summarizing long conversations, and in higher-tier plans operating with a degree of autonomy across dozens of concurrent agents. Native mobile apps for iOS and Android include a business-card scanner for quickly digitizing leads captured at in-person events, and the platform integrates with a large marketplace of third-party apps, payment gateways and e-commerce tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kommo prices per user per month across four tiers with a minimum six-month billing commitment: Base at $15, Advanced at $25, Pro at $45, and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier. Each step up increases the active-lead cap per user (2,500 to 5,000 to 10,000), the number of connected WhatsApp/Instagram accounts, and the depth of automation and AI features available, with Salesbot and broadcast messaging unlocked starting at Advanced and the full AI agent kit and analytics suite reserved for Pro and Enterprise.

All plans come with a 14-day free trial that requires no credit card and gives full access to every feature, limited only by 10GB of trial media storage. There is no permanent free plan, so teams that want to keep using Kommo beyond the trial must commit to one of the paid tiers, generally billed six months at a time.

faqs.question: What was Kommo previously called?
faqs.answer: Kommo was known as amoCRM until it rebranded in October 2022; the product, team and pricing carried over unchanged.

faqs.question: Does Kommo have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, Kommo does not offer a permanent free plan, but every paid tier includes a 14-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required.

faqs.question: How much does Kommo cost?
faqs.answer: Kommo starts at $15 per user per month for the Base plan, rising to $25 for Advanced and $45 for Pro, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier, all billed on a minimum six-month cycle.

faqs.question: Does Kommo integrate with WhatsApp?
faqs.answer: Yes, WhatsApp is one of Kommo's core native channels, integrated directly into the unified inbox alongside Instagram, Messenger, Telegram, email and web chat.

faqs.question: What is Salesbot in Kommo?
faqs.answer: Salesbot is Kommo's no-code chatbot builder that lets teams design automated conversation flows for lead qualification, follow-up and appointment booking.

faqs.question: Who is Kommo best suited for?
faqs.answer: Kommo is best suited for small and mid-sized sales, marketing and support teams that do a large share of their selling over messaging apps like WhatsApp and Instagram.

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered customer support platform combining live chat, an AI chatbot builder and human handoff across web, mobile and messaging channels.
enrichment.seo_title: Kommunicate Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kommunicate combines AI chatbots, live chat and human handoff into one support platform. See its pricing tiers, features and top competitors for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kommunicate is a customer support automation platform founded in 2020 by Devashish Mamgain and Adarsh Kumar, who previously built and led Applozic, a chat-infrastructure startup Mamgain co-founded in 2015 and ran as CEO until 2020. The company is headquartered in Bengaluru, India, and has grown into an AI-plus-human customer service product used by support teams that want chatbot automation without losing the ability to hand off to a live agent when a conversation gets complex. Architecturally, Kommunicate sits on top of a single AI agent layer that can be deployed across a website widget, native mobile apps, WhatsApp, Telegram, Instagram and email, so a business configures its bot logic and knowledge base once and it operates consistently across every channel a customer might reach out from. The platform's chatbot builder, marketed as Kompose, lets non-technical teams design conversational flows and connect a knowledge base for instant, AI-generated answers to common questions, while a human handoff mechanism routes conversations to live agents inside the same inbox whenever the bot cannot resolve an issue or a customer explicitly requests a person, preserving full conversation context in the transition. Around this core, Kommunicate layers rich messaging support (images, videos, quick replies), campaign messaging for proactive outreach, AI-generated conversation summaries, an auto-resolve feature that closes out simple tickets automatically, and an agent-assist capability that surfaces suggested replies to human support staff to speed up response times. Kommunicate prices in three tiers. The Starter plan costs $40 per month (or $34 per month billed annually) and includes one AI agent, one team member, 250 conversations per month (roughly 10,000 messages), integrations with web, mobile apps, WhatsApp, Telegram and Instagram, three months of chat history, and additional conversations billed at $15 per 1,000; extra AI agents or team members can be added for $20 per month each. The Professional plan, marked as the most popular tier, costs $200 per month (or $167 per month annually) and steps up to two AI agents, three team members, 2,000 conversations per month (roughly 80,000 messages), a full year of chat history, integrations with Zendesk, Freshdesk and Google Analytics, AI-generated conversation summaries, auto-resolve, agent assist, campaign messaging, API and webhook access, and cheaper overage pricing at $10 per 1,000 additional conversations, with priority support. The Enterprise tier is custom-priced and adds unlimited or custom-scaled AI agents and seats, discounted per-conversation pricing, deeper integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot and Zapier, a dedicated account manager, SLA-backed support, advanced analytics, single sign-on and identity management, and the ability to remove Kommunicate branding. Every plan, including Enterprise inquiries, is preceded by a 30-day free trial that requires no credit card. Kommunicate targets small to mid-market support teams — e-commerce brands, SaaS companies and service businesses — that want to deflect a meaningful share of routine support volume to AI while keeping a clear escalation path to human agents, and it particularly appeals to teams already using help desks like Zendesk or Freshdesk that want to layer AI chat on top rather than replace their existing ticketing system. Its most direct competitors are larger, more expensive conversational support platforms such as Intercom and Zendesk's own AI agent products, mid-market live-chat tools like Freshchat, Tidio and Drift, and open-source or developer-first alternatives like Chatwoot. Kommunicate differentiates itself primarily on price relative to Intercom and Zendesk, on the simplicity of its single AI-agent-across-all-channels architecture versus stitching together separate bot and live-chat tools, and on its founder team's decade of experience building chat infrastructure specifically (through the earlier Applozic project), which shows up in the platform's emphasis on omnichannel messaging reliability over broader helpdesk or ticketing feature sprawl.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kommunicate is a customer support platform that pairs an AI chatbot with live-agent handoff so businesses can automate routine questions while keeping a clear path to a human for anything the bot cannot handle. Founded in 2020 by Devashish Mamgain and Adarsh Kumar, a team with prior experience building chat infrastructure, the company is based in Bengaluru, India, and serves support teams that want AI automation layered onto channels they already use rather than a wholesale platform replacement.

The product runs on a single AI agent layer that is deployed consistently across a website widget, mobile apps, WhatsApp, Telegram, Instagram and email, so a business configures its bot's knowledge base and flows once and it behaves the same everywhere a customer might message in. When the bot cannot resolve a query, the conversation hands off to a live agent inside the same inbox with full context preserved.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of Kommunicate is its AI chatbot builder (Kompose), which lets non-technical teams design conversation flows and connect a knowledge base for instant answers, alongside a human handoff system that routes unresolved conversations to live support staff without losing context. Rich messaging support covers images, videos and quick replies across every connected channel.

Higher-tier plans add AI-generated conversation summaries, an auto-resolve feature for closing simple tickets automatically, agent-assist suggestions for human reps, and proactive campaign messaging for outbound engagement. Integrations extend into existing helpdesk stacks, connecting with Zendesk, Freshdesk and Google Analytics on the Professional plan and Salesforce, HubSpot and Zapier on Enterprise, plus API and webhook access for custom workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kommunicate's pricing scales primarily along three axes: number of AI agents, number of team members, and monthly conversation volume. Starter costs $40 per month for one AI agent, one seat and 250 conversations; Professional costs $200 per month for two AI agents, three seats and 2,000 conversations plus deeper integrations and automation features; and Enterprise is custom-priced with unlimited scale, dedicated support and SSO.

Annual billing discounts every tier by roughly 20 percent, bringing Starter to $34 per month and Professional to $167 per month. All plans include a 30-day free trial with no credit card required, and additional conversations beyond a plan's included volume are billed per thousand at rates that improve on higher tiers.

faqs.answer: Kommunicate supports website chat, native mobile apps, WhatsApp, Telegram, Instagram and email through a single AI agent layer.

faqs.question: Does Kommunicate offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, every plan includes a 30-day free trial that does not require a credit card.

faqs.question: How much does Kommunicate cost?
faqs.answer: Kommunicate starts at $40 per month for the Starter plan, with a Professional plan at $200 per month and a custom-priced Enterprise tier; annual billing saves roughly 20 percent.

faqs.question: Can Kommunicate hand off from bot to human agent?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform is built around seamless handoff from the AI chatbot to a live agent within the same inbox, preserving conversation context.

faqs.question: Does Kommunicate integrate with Zendesk or Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Zendesk and Freshdesk integrations are available on the Professional plan, while Salesforce, HubSpot and Zapier integrations are part of the Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: Who founded Kommunicate?
faqs.answer: Kommunicate was founded in 2020 by Devashish Mamgain and Adarsh Kumar, who previously built the chat-infrastructure startup Applozic.

enrichment.short_description: API and AI connectivity platform built around the open-source Kong Gateway, offering API management, an AI gateway and a Konnect control plane.
enrichment.seo_title: Kong Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kong is an open-source API gateway and AI connectivity platform used by enterprise dev teams. Compare Kong's features, Konnect pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kong Inc. traces its roots to 2009, when Augusto Marietti, Marco Palladino and Michele Zonca founded Mashape in Milan, Italy, originally as an API marketplace conceived after the founders demoed an early prototype at TechCrunch 50 in San Francisco. In 2015 Mashape open-sourced an internal API gateway project called Kong, and as the gateway gained far more traction than the marketplace itself, the company sold its API marketplace business to RapidAPI and rebranded entirely to Kong Inc. in October 2017, relocating its headquarters to San Francisco, California, where Marietti serves as CEO and Palladino as CTO. Kong has since raised roughly $345 million across multiple funding rounds, including a $175 million Series E led by Tiger Global Management and co-led by Balderton Capital with participation from existing investors Index Ventures, Andreessen Horowitz, CRV, Sapphire Ventures and Notable Capital, reaching a valuation of about $2 billion; the company reported crossing $100 million in annual recurring revenue and has grown to roughly 800-900 employees. Architecturally, Kong's product line centers on Kong Gateway, a lightweight, plugin-extensible API gateway built on Nginx and OpenResty (using Lua for its plugin system) that handles routing, authentication, rate limiting, load balancing and traffic transformation for REST, HTTP, gRPC, GraphQL, Kafka and WebSocket traffic; the open-source Community Edition of Kong Gateway remains free and self-hostable. On top of the gateway, Kong Konnect is the company's SaaS control plane and management layer, adding a developer portal for publishing and documenting APIs, centralized analytics, a service catalog, role-based access control and, more recently, a dedicated AI Gateway that routes and governs calls to large language models from providers like OpenAI, Anthropic and others, applying rate limiting, token quotas, prompt governance and semantic caching to AI traffic the same way the core gateway governs conventional API traffic. Kong also owns Kong Mesh (a service-mesh product built on Kuma) and Kong Insomnia, a widely used API client and testing tool for building and debugging API requests. Pricing follows a freemium structure: Kong Gateway's open-source edition is free and unlimited to self-host; Konnect offers a 30-day free trial with full enterprise functionality and no gateway limits; the Konnect Plus tier is billed per gateway and includes up to five serverless gateways, up to two hybrid or dedicated cloud gateways, an AI Gateway with access to five LLM models, up to two developer portals covering 20 published APIs, one million monthly analytics requests, role-based access control and email support with a two-business-day SLA; and Konnect Enterprise is custom-priced and billed annually, offering unlimited gateways, unlimited developer portals and published APIs, single sign-on, audit logs, dedicated customer success managers and custom support SLAs. Kong Insomnia is priced separately with a free Essentials tier (Git sync for up to three users, unlimited local/cloud projects), a Pro tier at $12 per user per month, and an Enterprise tier at $45 per user per month adding SSO/SCIM and Konnect integration. Kong's customers are primarily platform, DevOps and API teams at mid-market to large enterprises that need to manage large numbers of internal and external APIs, microservices and, increasingly, AI model traffic at scale, spanning industries from financial services to e-commerce and telecommunications. Kong's most direct competitors include Google's Apigee (strongest for teams already committed to Google Cloud, with built-in API monetization), Tyk (an open-source Go-based gateway that keeps more features in its free core), Gravitee (another open-source-first full-lifecycle API management platform), AWS API Gateway (the default choice for serverless APIs already living on AWS), and lighter-weight, performance-focused gateways like KrakenD and API7/Apache APISIX. Kong differentiates itself through its large plugin ecosystem, its dual open-source-plus-managed-SaaS model that lets teams start free and scale into Konnect, and its early, aggressive push into AI Gateway functionality aimed at teams that need to govern both traditional API traffic and LLM traffic through the same control plane.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kong began life in 2009 as Mashape, an API marketplace founded in Milan, Italy, by Augusto Marietti, Marco Palladino and Michele Zonca. The team open-sourced an internal API gateway named Kong in 2015, and as that gateway eclipsed the marketplace in importance, the company sold off the marketplace business and rebranded entirely to Kong Inc. in 2017, moving its headquarters to San Francisco.

Today Kong describes itself as an API and AI connectivity company. Its core open-source Kong Gateway handles routing, authentication and traffic management for APIs, while its Konnect SaaS platform layers on a developer portal, analytics, governance and, more recently, an AI Gateway for managing and securing traffic to large language models alongside conventional API traffic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kong Gateway is a plugin-extensible, Nginx/OpenResty-based API gateway that supports REST, HTTP, gRPC, GraphQL, Kafka and WebSocket protocols, handling authentication, rate limiting, load balancing and request/response transformation. Its open-source Community Edition is free to self-host and forms the foundation of Kong's entire product line.

Kong Konnect adds a hosted control plane on top of the gateway: a developer portal for publishing API documentation, centralized analytics across all gateways, a service catalog, role-based access control, and an AI Gateway that applies rate limiting, token quotas and prompt governance to LLM traffic from providers like OpenAI and Anthropic. The company also offers Kong Mesh for service-mesh use cases and Kong Insomnia, a standalone API client for designing and testing API requests.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kong follows a freemium model: the open-source Kong Gateway is free to self-host with no functional limits, and Konnect offers a 30-day free trial with full enterprise functionality. Beyond the trial, Konnect Plus is billed per gateway and includes up to five serverless gateways, an AI Gateway supporting five LLM models, and one million monthly analytics requests, while Konnect Enterprise is custom-priced and billed annually with unlimited gateways, SSO and dedicated customer success support.

Kong Insomnia, the company's API client, is priced separately from the gateway products, with a free Essentials tier, a $12-per-user-per-month Pro tier, and a $45-per-user-per-month Enterprise tier that adds SSO/SCIM and Konnect integration.

Unified platform for both traditional API traffic and AI/LLM traffic governance
Large plugin ecosystem covers most authentication, security and transformation needs
30-day free trial of Konnect gives full enterprise functionality before committing
Backed by significant funding and a mature enterprise support organization
cons: Konnect's per-gateway pricing can become expensive as API programs scale

Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales conversation

Some advanced governance features are gated behind the Enterprise tier only

faqs.question: Is Kong Gateway free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kong Gateway's Community Edition is open source and free to self-host with no functional limits.

faqs.question: What is Kong Konnect?
faqs.answer: Kong Konnect is Kong's hosted SaaS control plane that adds a developer portal, centralized analytics, service catalog and AI Gateway on top of Kong Gateway.

faqs.question: Does Kong support AI and LLM traffic?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kong's AI Gateway routes and governs calls to large language models, applying rate limiting, token quotas and prompt governance similar to how it manages conventional API traffic.

faqs.question: Who founded Kong?
faqs.answer: Kong originated as Mashape, founded in 2009 in Milan by Augusto Marietti, Marco Palladino and Michele Zonca, and rebranded to Kong Inc. in 2017.

faqs.question: How much does Kong Konnect cost?
faqs.answer: Konnect offers a 30-day free trial, then a per-gateway-priced Plus tier and a custom-priced, annually billed Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: What is Kong Insomnia?
faqs.answer: Kong Insomnia is a separate API client and testing tool for designing and debugging API requests, priced independently from the gateway with free, Pro and Enterprise tiers.

use_cases.title: Enterprise API management
use_cases.description: Platform teams use Kong Gateway and Konnect to manage authentication, rate limiting and analytics across hundreds of internal and external APIs.

use_cases.title: AI traffic governance

use_cases.title: Microservices connectivity

Unlimited developer portals and published APIs

enrichment.short_description: No-code visual workflow automation platform connecting over 800 apps to move data and automate tasks without writing code.
enrichment.seo_title: KonnectzIT Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KonnectzIT is a no-code automation platform connecting 800+ apps. See its pricing plans, workflow builder features and how it compares to Zapier and Pabbly.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KonnectzIT is a no-code workflow automation platform founded in 2020 that positions itself as a visual builder for connecting web applications and automating repetitive, multi-step business tasks without requiring any coding knowledge. The company is an independently operated, self-funded (unfunded, per public company databases) automation vendor whose team is reported to span India and Estonia, and it markets itself directly at founders, business owners and operations staff who need to move data between tools but do not have engineering resources to build custom integrations. Architecturally, KonnectzIT centers on a graphical, drag-and-drop workflow editor where users chain together triggers and actions across connected applications; unlike simple single-step 'if this then that' automations, the platform supports multi-step, rules-based workflows with conditional branching logic, scheduled/time-based triggers, and the ability to combine multiple apps and data transformations within a single automated flow (which the company refers to as a 'konnect'). The platform connects to more than 800 third-party applications spanning categories including CRM, email marketing, payments, cloud storage, e-commerce, and team communication, with commonly cited integrations including Google Sheets, Gmail, Slack, ActiveCampaign, ConvertKit/Kit and various e-commerce and CRM systems, and it also exposes its own API and webhook support so developers can extend workflows into custom or in-house systems that are not part of the pre-built connector library. KonnectzIT's pricing is built around a monthly task-execution quota rather than a flat per-integration fee, following a freemium structure: a permanently Free plan includes up to 100 tasks per month with access to all core platform functions, making it usable for very light automation needs at no cost; a Team plan at $15 per month includes 15,000 tasks per month, unlimited workflows and the ability to invite up to two team members; a Pro plan starts at $19 per month aimed at freelancers and small businesses needing a somewhat larger task allowance; and an Agency plan at $50 per month includes 50,000 tasks per month, five sub-accounts (useful for agencies managing automations on behalf of multiple clients), and unlimited workflows. Customers who commit to annual billing receive a 20 percent discount across all paid tiers. This tiered, task-based structure is designed to undercut better-known automation platforms on price while still supporting complex, branching workflows rather than only simple linear automations. KonnectzIT's target audience is primarily solo founders, small business owners, marketing operations staff, and small agencies that need to automate lead routing, data syncing between a CRM and an email tool, e-commerce order processing, or internal notification workflows, but who find that mainstream automation tools like Zapier become cost-prohibitive once task volume grows. The agency-specific sub-account feature in particular signals that KonnectzIT is courting marketing and operations agencies that manage automation infrastructure for multiple clients from one login. In the broader no-code automation category, KonnectzIT's most direct competitors are Zapier, the dominant and most expensive incumbent with the largest app directory; Make (formerly Integromat), which offers a similarly visual, more advanced workflow canvas favored by power users; Pabbly Connect, a lower-cost competitor that, like KonnectzIT, markets itself heavily on affordability and lifetime-deal pricing; and n8n, an open-source, self-hostable alternative aimed at technically inclined teams who want full control over their automation infrastructure. KonnectzIT differentiates itself primarily on price, offering a genuinely usable free tier and materially cheaper paid tiers than Zapier for comparable task volumes, while still supporting multi-step, rules-based, branching workflows rather than restricting free and low-tier users to simple linear automations, which is a common limitation among budget competitors in this space.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KonnectzIT is a no-code automation platform, founded in 2020, that lets businesses connect over 800 web applications and automate multi-step workflows through a visual, drag-and-drop builder rather than writing custom integration code. It is positioned as a more affordable alternative to established automation tools like Zapier while still supporting conditional, rules-based logic rather than only simple linear triggers.

The platform is aimed at founders, small business owners and operations teams who need to move data between CRMs, email marketing tools, payment systems and communication apps without engineering support, as well as agencies that manage automation for multiple clients through dedicated sub-accounts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of KonnectzIT is a graphical workflow editor that lets users chain triggers and actions across connected apps into what the platform calls a 'konnect,' supporting rules-based conditional branching and scheduled, time-based triggers rather than only simple one-step automations. The connector library spans more than 800 applications across CRM, email marketing, payments, storage and communication categories.

For workflows that fall outside the pre-built connector library, KonnectzIT exposes API and webhook access so developers can extend automations into custom or in-house systems. Agency-focused customers get access to sub-accounts, letting them manage separate automation workspaces for multiple clients from a single login.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KonnectzIT prices around monthly task-execution volume rather than a flat per-integration fee. A permanently free plan includes 100 tasks per month with access to core functionality, while paid tiers scale from a $15-per-month Team plan (15,000 tasks, up to 2 members) through a $19-per-month Pro plan up to a $50-per-month Agency plan offering 50,000 tasks, five sub-accounts and unlimited workflows.

Annual billing brings a 20 percent discount across every paid tier. This task-based, tiered pricing model is designed to make KonnectzIT noticeably cheaper than Zapier at comparable automation volumes while still allowing multi-step, branching workflows even on lower-priced plans.

Significantly cheaper than Zapier at comparable task volumes
Supports multi-step, rules-based branching workflows even on lower tiers
Agency plan with sub-accounts suits businesses managing automations for multiple clients
800+ pre-built app integrations cover most common business tools
cons: Smaller app directory and community than market leaders like Zapier or Make
Task-based pricing can be harder to predict than flat-rate competitors for variable workloads
Company headquarters and team size are not clearly published, limiting enterprise buyer confidence
Less brand recognition and third-party documentation than larger automation platforms
Advanced workflow debugging and observability tools are less mature than more established competitors

faqs.question: Is KonnectzIT free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, KonnectzIT offers a permanently free plan that includes up to 100 tasks per month with access to core platform functionality.

faqs.question: How much does KonnectzIT cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $15 per month for the Team plan (15,000 tasks) and go up to $50 per month for the Agency plan (50,000 tasks, 5 sub-accounts), with a 20 percent discount for annual billing.

faqs.question: How many apps does KonnectzIT integrate with?
faqs.answer: KonnectzIT connects to more than 800 third-party applications across categories including CRM, email marketing, payments, storage and communication.

faqs.question: Does KonnectzIT support conditional logic in workflows?
faqs.answer: Yes, the platform supports rules-based branching logic and scheduled triggers, not just simple one-step automations.

faqs.question: Who is KonnectzIT for?
faqs.answer: KonnectzIT is aimed at founders, small business owners, operations staff and agencies who need to automate workflows without engineering resources.

faqs.question: How does KonnectzIT compare to Zapier?
faqs.answer: KonnectzIT positions itself as a lower-cost alternative to Zapier, offering a usable free tier and cheaper paid tiers while still supporting multi-step, branching workflows.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, cross-platform backup tool with client-side encryption, compression and deduplication, usable via CLI or GUI.
enrichment.seo_title: Kopia Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kopia is a free, open-source backup tool with end-to-end encryption and deduplication. Compare its features, repository options and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Kopia is a free and open-source cross-platform backup and restore tool led by Jarek Kowalski, a software engineer at Google with a background in distributed systems, Kubernetes and storage, who created the project and continues to serve as its lead developer. The project is hosted publicly on GitHub under an open-source license and has drawn ongoing engineering support and sponsorship from Kasten by Veeam, the Kubernetes-native data protection division that Veeam acquired in a deal reportedly worth around $150 million in 2020; Kasten uses Kopia internally for Kubernetes backup workflows and its engineers, including R&D technical leader Julio Lopez, have contributed to and helped sustain the project's development. Because Kopia is a community open-source tool rather than a venture-backed company, it has no headquarters, funding rounds or employee count in the traditional sense; its 'organization' is effectively its maintainer team and contributor community. Architecturally, Kopia works by creating snapshots of designated files and directories, encrypting that data client-side (on the user's own machine) before it ever leaves the device, and then uploading the encrypted, deduplicated snapshot to a storage location the user controls, referred to as a repository. Kopia deliberately does not support creating unencrypted backups at all — every snapshot is protected end-to-end using user-controlled encryption keys, meaning neither the storage provider nor any third party can read the backed-up content or even the file and directory names without the user's key. Supported repository backends include Amazon S3 and other S3-compatible object storage services, Azure Blob Storage, Backblaze B2, Google Cloud Storage, remote servers or cloud storage accessible via WebDAV or SFTP, and purely local storage on network-attached storage devices or the user's own machine. On top of encryption, Kopia performs content-based deduplication, meaning identical file content is stored only once across an entire repository even if it appears in multiple backups or is contributed by multiple users or computers sharing the same repository, alongside compression and error-correction features that together minimize both storage costs and the risk of silent data corruption. The tool ships with both a command-line interface for scripting and automation, and a graphical desktop application (KopiaUI) for users who prefer a visual interface to configure snapshot policies, review backup history and restore files, with both interfaces available natively on Windows, macOS and Linux. Kopia supports configurable snapshot policies including retention rules, scheduling, and exclusion filters, making it suitable for both one-off manual backups and fully automated, recurring backup regimes. Because the software itself is entirely free and open source, Kopia's only real cost to users is whatever storage backend they choose to pair it with (for example, S3 or Backblaze B2 storage fees), and there is no separate 'Kopia' subscription, license fee or paid tier of any kind. This makes Kopia particularly attractive to privacy-conscious individuals, self-hosters, sysadmins and small teams who want enterprise-grade encryption and deduplication without paying for a commercial backup service, as well as to organizations like Kasten that embed Kopia as backup infrastructure inside larger commercial products. Kopia's closest competitors in the open-source backup space are Restic, a similarly encrypted, deduplicating, CLI-first backup tool with a large community; Borg Backup (BorgBackup), another mature deduplicating backup tool popular among Linux and self-hosting communities; Duplicati, which offers a more consumer-friendly GUI-first experience with built-in scheduling; and Duplicacy, a lock-free deduplication tool with both open-source and commercial licensing options. Kopia differentiates itself from these alternatives primarily through its combination of a genuinely polished graphical interface (KopiaUI) alongside its CLI, broad native cross-platform support without relying on external tools, and its growing adoption inside Kubernetes-native backup infrastructure through the Kasten/Veeam relationship, which gives the project a level of ongoing corporate engineering investment that purely community-driven competitors like Restic and Borg do not have to the same degree.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kopia is a free, open-source backup and restore tool created and led by Google engineer Jarek Kowalski, designed to create encrypted, deduplicated snapshots of files and directories and store them in a repository of the user's choosing. Because it is a community open-source project rather than a company, it has no pricing, funding or headquarters — the software itself is entirely free to use.

The project receives ongoing engineering support from Kasten by Veeam, the Kubernetes-native backup division Veeam acquired in 2020, which uses Kopia internally for Kubernetes backup workflows and contributes to its continued development. This gives Kopia a level of sustained corporate investment that is unusual among community-maintained open-source backup tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Kopia's defining feature is that it never creates unencrypted backups: every snapshot is encrypted client-side with user-controlled keys before leaving the device, protecting both file contents and file/directory names. Alongside encryption, Kopia performs content-based deduplication so identical data is stored only once across a repository, plus compression and error correction to minimize storage costs and guard against corruption.

Repository backends include Amazon S3 and S3-compatible storage, Azure Blob Storage, Backblaze B2, Google Cloud Storage, WebDAV/SFTP-accessible servers, and local or network-attached storage. Kopia ships with both a command-line interface for automation and scripting and a graphical desktop app (KopiaUI) for visual configuration, snapshot review and restores, with native support on Windows, macOS and Linux.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Kopia itself is completely free and open source, with no license fees, subscriptions or paid tiers of any kind. The only cost users incur is for whichever storage backend they choose to pair with Kopia, such as Amazon S3, Backblaze B2 or Azure Blob Storage fees, which are billed directly by those providers rather than by the Kopia project.

This makes Kopia's total cost of ownership entirely a function of the storage a user selects and how much data they back up, rather than any fee tied to the backup software itself, distinguishing it from commercial backup services that charge per-device or per-user subscription fees on top of storage costs.

features.title: Client-side end-to-end encryption
features.description: All snapshots are encrypted on the user's device with user-controlled keys before upload; unencrypted backups are not supported.

features.title: Content-based deduplication
features.description: Identical file content is stored only once across a repository, even across multiple machines or users.

features.title: Compression and error correction
features.description: Reduces storage footprint and protects against silent data corruption in stored snapshots.

features.title: Multiple repository backends
features.description: Supports Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Backblaze B2, Google Cloud Storage, WebDAV/SFTP and local/NAS storage.

features.title: CLI and GUI (KopiaUI)
features.description: Command-line interface for automation plus a native graphical desktop app for visual backup management.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs natively on Windows, macOS and Linux.

features.title: Configurable snapshot policies
features.description: Set retention rules, scheduling and exclusion filters for automated recurring backups.

features.title: Shared repository deduplication
features.description: Multiple users or computers can share one repository, deduplicating shared files across them.

pros: Completely free and open source with no license or subscription fees
Strong end-to-end encryption enforced by design, not optional
Both CLI and polished GUI available for different user preferences
Wide range of supported storage backends including S3, B2, Azure and local NAS
Backed by ongoing engineering investment from Kasten by Veeam
cons: No commercial support contract or SLA available directly from a vendor
Users must bring and pay for their own storage backend separately
Smaller community and ecosystem than more established tools like Restic or Borg
Advanced configuration (policies, repository tuning) has a learning curve for new users
No built-in cloud storage of its own, requiring third-party backend setup

faqs.question: Is Kopia free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kopia is completely free and open source with no license fees or paid tiers; users only pay for their chosen storage backend.

faqs.question: Does Kopia encrypt backups?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kopia encrypts all data client-side with user-controlled keys before it leaves the device, and does not support creating unencrypted backups.

faqs.question: What storage backends does Kopia support?
faqs.answer: Kopia supports Amazon S3 and S3-compatible services, Azure Blob Storage, Backblaze B2, Google Cloud Storage, WebDAV/SFTP servers, and local or network-attached storage.

faqs.question: Does Kopia have a graphical interface?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kopia includes both a command-line interface and a native graphical desktop app called KopiaUI, available on Windows, macOS and Linux.

faqs.question: Who maintains Kopia?
faqs.answer: Kopia is led by Google engineer Jarek Kowalski and receives ongoing development support from Kasten by Veeam, which uses Kopia for Kubernetes backup workflows.

faqs.question: Does Kopia support deduplication?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kopia performs content-based deduplication so identical file content is stored only once across a repository, even when shared across multiple machines.

use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals use Kopia to back up personal files to encrypted cloud or local storage.

use_cases.title: Kubernetes-native data protection
use_cases.description: Platforms like Kasten by Veeam embed Kopia as backup infrastructure for Kubernetes workloads.

use_cases.title: Small team shared backup repositories
use_cases.description: Small teams share a single deduplicated repository across multiple machines to save on storage costs.

enrichment.short_description: Serverless cloud platform for deploying apps, APIs and AI inference workloads on CPUs and GPUs with per-second billing and scale-to-zero.
enrichment.company_size: 13 employees (at time of Mistral AI acquisition, Feb 2026)
enrichment.seo_title: Koyeb Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Koyeb is a serverless cloud platform for deploying apps, APIs and GPU AI workloads. See pricing, GPU options and how it compares to Render and Fly.io.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Koyeb is a serverless cloud infrastructure platform founded in 2020 by Yann Léger, Édouard Bonlieu and Bastien Chatelard, three former employees of the French cloud provider Scaleway who set out to let developers deploy and run applications without managing underlying server infrastructure. The company is headquartered in Paris, France, and raised approximately $8.6 million to $16 million across its funding history, including a $1.6 million pre-seed round in 2020 and a $7 million seed round in 2023 led by Paris-based VC firm Serena with participation from Bertrand Diard, Floriane de Maupeou, ISAI, Samsung Next and MongoDB. In February 2026, Mistral AI announced its acquisition of Koyeb — Mistral's first acquisition to date — bringing Koyeb's roughly 13 employees and three co-founders into Mistral's engineering organization under CTO and co-founder Timothée Lacroix, a move explicitly aimed at accelerating Mistral Compute, the AI lab's cloud infrastructure ambitions announced in mid-2025, and positioning Mistral as a full-stack AI company rather than solely a model developer. Architecturally, Koyeb operates as a fully managed, globally distributed serverless platform: developers deploy applications, APIs, background workers and AI inference workloads directly from a Git repository or a Docker image, and Koyeb handles autoscaling, load balancing, TLS, and geographic distribution across its edge network, with services able to scale to zero when idle and scale back up on demand, billed per second of actual compute usage rather than pre-reserved capacity. Beyond general-purpose compute, Koyeb has built out dedicated support for AI and machine learning workloads, offering on-demand access to NVIDIA GPUs spanning the RTX 4000 SFF, A100, H100, H200 and B200, priced from roughly $0.50 per hour up to $24 per hour for an 8x H200 configuration, all billed per second with no minimum commitment, making it a viable platform for running model inference and fine-tuning jobs without provisioning dedicated GPU infrastructure. The platform also offers a managed, serverless Postgres database product with autoscaling storage priced at $0.50 per GB per month and compute instances ranging from a free tier up to $1.28 per hour. Koyeb's pricing spans four tiers: a free Hobby plan restricted to one free web service (512MB RAM, 0.1 vCPU, 2GB SSD) bundled with a small free Postgres database instance, intended for previews and small hobby projects rather than production traffic, requiring no credit card and never expiring; a Pro plan at $29 per month that includes $10 of compute credit, support for up to 10 users and 100 services, NVMe volumes, up to 5 concurrent builds and email/chat support; a Scale plan at $299 per month including $100 of compute credit, support for up to 50 users and 1,000 services, access to additional AWS regions, Slack integration and a 99.9 percent uptime SLA; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan starting around $1,000 per month with $500 of compute credit, unlimited users, private dedicated locations, SSO/RBAC, a 99.99 percent uptime SLA and 24/7 premium support. Koyeb also runs an early-stage startup program offering up to $30,000 in platform credits, and offers committed-capacity discounts of up to 50 percent off on-demand pricing for teams willing to commit to long-term reservations. Koyeb's target customers are developers and engineering teams building APIs, web backends, background workers and, increasingly, AI inference services who want the simplicity of a serverless deployment model (deploy from Git or Docker, get autoscaling and global distribution for free) without the operational overhead of managing Kubernetes clusters or raw cloud VMs themselves, ranging from solo developers on the free Hobby tier to funded startups needing GPU access for production AI workloads. Koyeb's most direct competitors in the developer-platform-as-a-service category include Render, Fly.io and Railway, all of which offer similarly git-driven, autoscaling application deployment aimed at replacing raw cloud infrastructure management, as well as Vercel for frontend-focused workloads; in the GPU/AI-inference niche specifically, Koyeb competes with Modal, Replicate and, increasingly following its acquisition, its own new parent Mistral AI's broader compute ambitions. Koyeb differentiated itself pre-acquisition through aggressive per-second GPU billing with scale-to-zero, a genuinely free and non-expiring Hobby tier bundled with a free database, and now, post-acquisition, through direct integration into Mistral AI's infrastructure roadmap as Europe's answer to sovereign AI cloud infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Koyeb is a serverless cloud platform founded in 2020 by three former Scaleway employees, Yann Léger, Édouard Bonlieu and Bastien Chatelard, and headquartered in Paris, France. It lets developers deploy applications, APIs and AI inference workloads directly from Git or Docker without managing servers, with automatic scaling, global distribution and per-second billing.

In February 2026, Mistral AI acquired Koyeb as its first-ever acquisition, bringing the roughly 13-person team into Mistral's engineering organization to accelerate Mistral Compute, the AI lab's cloud infrastructure initiative, signaling Koyeb's evolution from an independent developer platform toward core AI infrastructure for one of Europe's leading AI labs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Koyeb's core platform deploys apps directly from a Git repository or Docker image and handles autoscaling, load balancing, TLS and global edge distribution automatically, with services able to scale to zero when idle and back up on demand, billed per second of actual usage. This makes it a git-push deployment experience without the operational burden of managing Kubernetes or raw VMs.

For AI and machine learning workloads, Koyeb offers on-demand NVIDIA GPU access spanning RTX 4000 SFF up through A100, H100, H200 and B200 configurations, all billed per second with no minimum commitment, alongside a managed serverless Postgres database product with autoscaling storage for applications that need a bundled data layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Koyeb offers a genuinely free Hobby plan (one web service plus a small Postgres instance, no credit card required, never expires) alongside three paid tiers: Pro at $29/month with $10 of compute credit, Scale at $299/month with $100 of compute credit and a 99.9% SLA, and a custom Enterprise plan starting around $1,000/month with $500 of compute credit, unlimited users and a 99.99% SLA.

GPU compute is billed separately and per second, ranging from about $0.50/hour for an RTX 4000 SFF up to $24/hour for an 8x H200 configuration, with committed-capacity discounts of up to 50% available for teams that reserve capacity long-term, and an early-stage startup program offering up to $30,000 in credits.

pros: Genuinely free, non-expiring Hobby plan with a bundled Postgres database
Per-second billing on GPUs makes AI inference workloads cost-efficient
Simple git-push deployment model without Kubernetes overhead
Access to high-end GPUs (H100, H200, B200) without long-term commitments
Now backed by Mistral AI's infrastructure investment following the 2026 acquisition
cons: Free Hobby tier is explicitly not intended for production traffic
Smaller regional footprint than hyperscalers like AWS, Azure or GCP
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a custom quote
Post-acquisition roadmap and independence from Mistral AI's priorities is still unfolding
Smaller team size relative to larger PaaS competitors could affect support responsiveness at scale

faqs.question: Does Koyeb have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koyeb's Hobby plan is free forever, includes one web service and a small Postgres database instance, requires no credit card, and never expires, though it is not intended for production traffic.

faqs.question: How much does Koyeb cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $29/month for Pro (with $10 compute credit), $299/month for Scale (with $100 compute credit and a 99.9% SLA), and custom Enterprise pricing starting around $1,000/month.

faqs.question: Does Koyeb support GPU workloads?
faqs.answer: Yes, Koyeb offers on-demand NVIDIA GPUs including RTX 4000, A100, H100, H200 and B200, billed per second starting around $0.50/hour.

faqs.question: Who owns Koyeb?
faqs.answer: Koyeb was acquired by Mistral AI in February 2026, its first acquisition, to accelerate Mistral's cloud infrastructure ambitions.

faqs.question: Where is Koyeb based?
faqs.answer: Koyeb is headquartered in Paris, France, and was founded in 2020 by three former Scaleway employees.

faqs.question: How does Koyeb billing work?
faqs.answer: Koyeb bills per second of actual compute usage, including GPU instances, with services able to scale to zero when idle.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, high-performance API gateway written in Go, offering stateless, declarative routing plus an Enterprise edition with AI Gateway and security features.
enrichment.seo_title: KrakenD Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: KrakenD is an open-source, high-performance API gateway written in Go. Compare its Community and Enterprise editions, AI Gateway features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: KrakenD is an open-source API gateway created in 2016 by software engineers Albert Lombarte, Daniel Lopez and Daniel Ortiz, with the KrakenD company formally incorporated in 2021 and headquartered in Barcelona, Spain. Written in Go, KrakenD was designed from the outset around a stateless, declarative configuration model rather than requiring plugin code for common gateway behaviors, aiming to deliver very high throughput and low latency for API traffic in distributed and cloud-native architectures. In 2021, the project's open-source core was contributed to the Linux Foundation and renamed the Lura Project, reflecting the framework's positioning as vendor-neutral open infrastructure even as the KrakenD company continued to build and sell a commercial Enterprise edition on top of it. The Community Edition (KrakenD CE) remains free and fully open source under the Apache 2.0 license, and covers the full core of an API gateway: declarative routing, rate limiting, request/response transformation and aggregation of multiple backend calls into a single API response, all configured through a JSON-based declarative file rather than custom code, which the company markets as eliminating the need for developers to write and maintain gateway-specific plugin logic for routine tasks. On top of the free core, KrakenD Enterprise Edition adds the features that production teams operating at scale typically require: single sign-on and SAML support, role-based access control, audit logging, immediate security patches, direct support with a service-level agreement from KrakenD's own engineering team, and protocol support for gRPC, WebSockets, API key management and OpenAPI specification integration. A significant recent addition to the Enterprise edition, introduced in the KrakenD EE 2.10 release, is an AI Gateway module bundled at no extra license cost, which enables secure routing and transformation of calls to multiple large language model providers including OpenAI, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini and Mistral, or custom self-hosted models; this AI Gateway layer enforces zero-trust architecture patterns such as isolated authorization flows, hidden upstream API keys, and request/response data masking to prevent sensitive data exfiltration, applies persistent token quotas and consumption-tier enforcement for cost control, and provides governance tooling including prompt validation, multi-LLM routing strategies and reusable prompt templates. Later Enterprise releases (2.12 and 2.13) extended this further with AI budget-control dashboards, AWS Bedrock support, Kafka integration and expanded plugin extensibility. KrakenD's pricing model for Enterprise is deliberately structured to be stable and predictable rather than usage-based: unlike some competitors that charge per API, per request or per throughput tier, KrakenD Enterprise pricing is not tied to the number of APIs or the volume of traffic passing through the gateway, and prospective customers can evaluate the full Enterprise feature set through a free two-month trial before committing to a custom quote, as exact Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting KrakenD's sales team. KrakenD's target customers are engineering and platform teams building high-throughput, low-latency API layers in front of microservices, particularly those who want the performance characteristics of a purpose-built Go gateway without the overhead of a heavier, plugin-driven architecture, as well as, increasingly, platform teams that need to govern and secure LLM/AI traffic using the same gateway infrastructure they already use for conventional APIs. In the API gateway market, KrakenD's closest competitors include Kong, the more feature-rich and widely adopted open-source-plus-SaaS gateway that also offers an AI Gateway but with a larger, more complex plugin ecosystem; Tyk, another open-source Go-based gateway that keeps more governance features in its free tier; Gravitee, a full-lifecycle API management platform with a similarly open-source-first philosophy; Apigee, Google's fully managed API management product aimed at teams committed to Google Cloud; and Amazon API Gateway, the default choice for teams building serverless APIs natively on AWS. KrakenD differentiates itself from these rivals primarily through raw performance benchmarks (it is frequently cited alongside API7/Apache APISIX as among the fastest gateways available), its declarative, no-plugin-code configuration philosophy that reduces operational complexity, its stable pricing model that is not linked to API count or throughput (unlike many competitors), and its comparatively early and deeply integrated AI Gateway functionality bundled directly into the Enterprise license rather than sold as a separate add-on product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: KrakenD is an open-source API gateway written in Go, created in 2016 by engineers Albert Lombarte, Daniel Lopez and Daniel Ortiz and formally incorporated as a company in 2021, headquartered in Barcelona, Spain. It was built around a stateless, declarative configuration model designed to deliver high throughput and low latency for API traffic without requiring custom plugin code for routine gateway behaviors.

The open-source core, KrakenD Community Edition, was contributed to the Linux Foundation in 2021 and renamed the Lura Project, while the KrakenD company continues to develop and sell a commercial Enterprise Edition that adds security, governance and, more recently, AI Gateway functionality on top of the free core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: KrakenD Community Edition covers the core functions of an API gateway: declarative, JSON-configured routing, rate limiting, request/response transformation and the ability to aggregate multiple backend API calls into a single response, all without requiring developers to write plugin code. The gateway is stateless by design, which contributes to its reputation for high performance in benchmarks against other gateways.

KrakenD Enterprise Edition adds SSO/SAML, role-based access control, audit logging, gRPC and WebSocket support, API key management, OpenAPI integration, and immediate security patches with SLA-backed support. Its AI Gateway module, bundled at no extra license cost, secures and governs traffic to LLM providers like OpenAI, Claude, Gemini and Mistral with token quotas, prompt validation, data masking and multi-LLM routing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: KrakenD Community Edition is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, covering the full core gateway functionality with no cost or usage limits. KrakenD Enterprise Edition is custom-priced and not published publicly, but the company markets its Enterprise pricing model as stable and predictable, explicitly not tied to the number of APIs managed or the volume of traffic processed.

Prospective Enterprise customers can trial the full feature set, including the AI Gateway, security policies and SSO, free for two months before committing to a paid contract, after which pricing is negotiated directly with KrakenD's sales team based on deployment scale and support needs.

features.title: Declarative, no-plugin-code configuration
features.description: Configure routing, rate limiting and transformations through JSON files instead of writing custom plugin code.

features.title: Stateless, high-performance architecture
features.description: Go-based stateless design built for high throughput and low latency API traffic.

features.title: Request aggregation
features.description: Combine multiple backend API calls into a single client-facing response.

features.title: AI Gateway
features.description: Secure, govern and route traffic to multiple LLM providers with token quotas and prompt guardrails, bundled in Enterprise.

features.title: SSO and RBAC
features.description: Single sign-on, SAML and role-based access control available on the Enterprise edition.

features.title: gRPC and WebSocket support
features.description: Enterprise edition adds protocol support beyond standard REST/HTTP.

features.title: Audit logging and security patches
features.description: Enterprise customers receive immediate security fixes and full audit trails.

features.title: Stable, usage-independent pricing
features.description: Enterprise pricing is not tied to the number of APIs or traffic throughput.

pros: Community Edition is fully free, open source and production-capable
Declarative configuration avoids the need for custom plugin development
Frequently benchmarked among the fastest available API gateways
AI Gateway features are bundled into Enterprise at no extra license cost
Enterprise pricing is not linked to API count or traffic volume, unlike many competitors
cons: Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires a sales conversation
Smaller plugin/extension ecosystem than more established competitors like Kong
Some advanced security and governance features are Enterprise-only
Declarative-only configuration can feel restrictive to teams wanting custom plugin logic
Smaller company size than larger API management vendors may affect long-term support depth

faqs.question: Is KrakenD free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, KrakenD Community Edition is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, covering core routing, rate limiting and transformation features.

faqs.question: What is included in KrakenD Enterprise?
faqs.answer: KrakenD Enterprise adds SSO/SAML, RBAC, audit logging, gRPC/WebSocket support, an AI Gateway for LLM traffic, and SLA-backed support with immediate security patches.

faqs.question: Does KrakenD support AI and LLM traffic?
faqs.answer: Yes, KrakenD Enterprise includes an AI Gateway module that routes and secures traffic to providers like OpenAI, Claude, Gemini and Mistral with token quotas and prompt governance.

faqs.question: How is KrakenD Enterprise priced?
faqs.answer: KrakenD Enterprise pricing is custom and not tied to the number of APIs or traffic throughput; a free two-month trial is available before committing to a quote.

faqs.question: Who created KrakenD?
faqs.answer: KrakenD was created in 2016 by Albert Lombarte, Daniel Lopez and Daniel Ortiz, with the company formally incorporated in Barcelona, Spain in 2021.

faqs.question: What is the Lura Project?
faqs.answer: The Lura Project is the name given to KrakenD's open-source core after it was contributed to the Linux Foundation in 2021.

use_cases.title: High-throughput microservices gateway
use_cases.description: Engineering teams use KrakenD in front of microservices to handle routing, rate limiting and aggregation at high performance.

use_cases.title: AI traffic governance
use_cases.description: Teams route and secure calls to multiple LLM providers through KrakenD's Enterprise AI Gateway.

use_cases.title: API response aggregation
use_cases.description: Teams use KrakenD to combine multiple backend calls into a single simplified API response for client apps.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, Laravel-based CRM for lead, deal and customer lifecycle management, built by the team behind Bagisto.
enrichment.seo_title: Krayin CRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Krayin CRM is a free, open-source Laravel CRM for lead and deal management. See its features, premium extensions and how it compares to SuiteCRM and Odoo.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Krayin CRM is an open-source customer relationship management platform launched in 2021 by Jitendra Singh, Saurav Pathak and Vipin Sahu, and developed by Webkul Software, an established Indian IT product company founded in 2010 and headquartered in Noida, Uttar Pradesh, India, with roughly 550 employees spread across Asia, North America and Africa as of 2026. Webkul is also the company behind Bagisto, a well-known open-source Laravel-based e-commerce platform, and Krayin CRM was built by the same engineering team using the same core technology stack, applying Bagisto's open-source, self-hosted philosophy to the CRM category. Architecturally, Krayin is built entirely on Laravel, the popular PHP web application framework, with Vue.js powering its frontend interface, making it a fully self-hostable application that businesses can deploy on their own servers or a cloud provider of choice rather than relying on a vendor-hosted SaaS instance, and giving developers already familiar with the Laravel ecosystem a natural path to customize or extend the CRM's codebase. The product covers the core functional areas expected of a modern CRM: lead capture and tracking, a visual deal pipeline for managing sales opportunities through configurable stages, sales forecasting, and tools for campaign management, email marketing and customer segmentation that let sales and marketing teams target specific groups of contacts with tailored messaging. Krayin also bundles functionality that many competing CRMs — including some commercial ones — reserve for paid tiers, such as a built-in product catalog and a quoting module for generating and sending sales quotes directly from the CRM, along with omnichannel customer engagement tools spanning VoIP calling, live chat and structured customer feedback collection. More recently, Krayin has begun incorporating AI-powered automation into its workflow engine, including AI-driven lead scoring to help sales teams prioritize the most promising opportunities, automated support-ticket classification, customer intent prediction, and an AI chatbot for handling routine inbound queries, extending the platform's original sales-pipeline focus into broader AI-assisted customer lifecycle management. Krayin's licensing model is entirely free: the core CRM is released under the MIT License, meaning businesses can download, self-host, modify and deploy it commercially without paying any licensing fee, and Webkul has publicly committed to keeping the core open-source framework free permanently. Revenue for Webkul instead comes from optional premium extensions sold through its online store on a one-time-purchase basis rather than a recurring subscription — for example, a WhatsApp integration extension is priced at $1,499 and a VoIP calling extension at $4,500 — as well as from paid implementation, customization and support services for businesses that want professional help deploying or extending Krayin, and from a multi-tenant SaaS module that lets agencies or larger businesses run Krayin as a hosted, white-labeled CRM product for their own customers. Krayin's target audience is primarily small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as developers and digital agencies, that want full ownership and control over their CRM data and infrastructure without paying per-user SaaS subscription fees, and that are comfortable with or actively prefer a self-hosted, Laravel-based technology stack that they can customize beyond what a closed commercial CRM would allow. Within the open-source CRM category, Krayin's most direct competitors are SuiteCRM and EspoCRM, both long-established, free, self-hostable CRM platforms with large plugin ecosystems, and Odoo's CRM module, which is open source at its core but typically sold as part of Odoo's broader paid business-suite subscription; among commercial SaaS CRMs, Krayin competes indirectly with Salesforce, HubSpot CRM, Zoho CRM and Vtiger for budget-conscious teams evaluating whether to self-host versus subscribe. Krayin differentiates itself primarily through its modern Laravel and Vue.js technology stack (which many PHP developers find more approachable and contemporary than the older codebases underlying SuiteCRM or EspoCRM), its MIT-licensed permanently free core, its inclusion of quoting and product-catalog functionality that other open-source CRMs often lack out of the box, and its backing by Webkul, an already-profitable, multi-product software company with an established track record supporting Bagisto, which gives Krayin a level of ongoing development and commercial support infrastructure that many purely community-driven open-source CRM projects do not have.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Krayin CRM is a free, open-source customer relationship management platform launched in 2021 by Jitendra Singh, Saurav Pathak and Vipin Sahu, and built by Webkul Software, an Indian IT company headquartered in Noida that is also known for the open-source e-commerce platform Bagisto. Krayin applies the same open-source, self-hosted philosophy to the CRM category, built entirely on Laravel with a Vue.js frontend.

Because Krayin is self-hosted and released under the MIT License, businesses can deploy, modify and use it commercially without any per-user licensing fee, distinguishing it from subscription-based commercial CRMs like Salesforce or HubSpot while still covering the core functional areas those platforms are known for.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Krayin covers lead capture and tracking, a visual deal pipeline with configurable stages, sales forecasting, and campaign management tools for email marketing and customer segmentation. It also bundles a product catalog and quoting module for generating sales quotes directly from the CRM, functionality that many competing open-source and commercial CRMs reserve for paid tiers.

More recent releases have added AI-powered automation, including AI-driven lead scoring, automated support-ticket classification, customer intent prediction and an AI chatbot for handling routine inquiries, alongside existing omnichannel tools like VoIP calling, live chat and structured customer feedback collection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Krayin CRM is entirely free and released under the MIT License, allowing businesses to self-host, modify and deploy it commercially without any licensing cost. Webkul monetizes the project instead through optional premium extensions sold as one-time purchases, such as a WhatsApp integration priced at $1,499 and a VoIP calling extension at $4,500.

Additional revenue comes from paid implementation, customization and support services for businesses that want professional help deploying Krayin, as well as a multi-tenant SaaS module that lets agencies run Krayin as a hosted, white-labeled CRM product for their own clients.

features.title: Lead and deal pipeline management
features.description: Track leads and manage sales opportunities through a configurable, visual deal pipeline.

features.title: Sales forecasting
features.description: Forecast sales performance based on pipeline and historical deal data.

features.title: Campaign and email marketing
features.description: Manage marketing campaigns and customer segmentation directly from the CRM.

features.title: Product catalog and quoting
features.description: Built-in product catalog and quoting module for generating sales quotes.

features.title: VoIP and live chat
features.description: Omnichannel customer engagement tools for calling and live chat support.

features.title: AI-powered automation
features.description: AI-driven lead scoring, ticket classification, intent prediction and chatbot support.

features.title: Self-hosted, open-source deployment
features.description: Fully self-hostable on Laravel with complete control over data and infrastructure.

features.title: Multi-tenant SaaS module
features.description: Optional paid module for agencies to run Krayin as a hosted, white-labeled CRM.

pros: Core CRM is entirely free under the MIT License with no per-user fees
Built on the modern, widely known Laravel and Vue.js stack for easy customization
Includes product catalog and quoting features often missing from other free CRMs
Backed by Webkul, an established software company with a track record supporting Bagisto
Full data ownership through self-hosting rather than a vendor-hosted SaaS instance
cons: Self-hosting requires technical resources to deploy, secure and maintain the server
Premium extensions like WhatsApp and VoIP integration carry significant one-time costs
Smaller plugin ecosystem than more established open-source CRMs like SuiteCRM
No official managed cloud-hosted version directly from Krayin for non-technical teams
Newer project (launched 2021) with a smaller long-term track record than older competitors

faqs.question: Is Krayin CRM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Krayin CRM is free and open source under the MIT License, with no per-user licensing fees for self-hosted deployment.

faqs.question: What technology is Krayin CRM built on?
faqs.answer: Krayin CRM is built on the Laravel PHP framework with a Vue.js frontend, the same stack used by Webkul's Bagisto e-commerce platform.

faqs.question: Who built Krayin CRM?
faqs.answer: Krayin CRM was launched in 2021 by Jitendra Singh, Saurav Pathak and Vipin Sahu, and is developed by Webkul Software, headquartered in Noida, India.

faqs.question: Does Krayin CRM have premium add-ons?
faqs.answer: Yes, Webkul sells optional one-time-purchase extensions such as WhatsApp integration ($1,499) and VoIP calling ($4,500) on top of the free core CRM.

faqs.question: Can Krayin CRM be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Krayin is designed to be fully self-hosted on a business's own servers or cloud provider rather than requiring a vendor-hosted SaaS subscription.

faqs.question: Does Krayin CRM support AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, recent versions include AI-powered lead scoring, support-ticket classification, customer intent prediction and an AI chatbot.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted sales pipeline management
use_cases.description: SMEs deploy Krayin on their own infrastructure to manage leads and deals without per-user SaaS fees.

use_cases.title: Agency white-label CRM
use_cases.description: Digital agencies use Krayin's multi-tenant SaaS module to offer a branded CRM product to their own clients.

use_cases.title: Custom CRM development
use_cases.description: Laravel developers extend Krayin's open-source codebase to build a fully customized CRM for specific business needs.
